entities.name: akṛtaka-śāstra
entities.cite: a teaching that is not made

entities.name: smṛti
entities.cite: the smrti is not arbitrary: it has nibandhana

entities.name: śabdānāmanuśāsana
entities.cite: the term echoes sabdanusasana, the traditional name of grammar itself

axioms.name: asritya
axioms.cite: proceeds akrtakam sastram asritya, resting on a teaching that is not made

axioms.name: nibandhana
axioms.cite: it has nibandhana, attachments to its source

axioms.name: asritya
axioms.cite: Sanibandhana, 'with its grounds' or 'bindings', concedes that the smrti is not arbitrary

claims.name: asritya
claims.cite: on the tradition with its grounds

rejected_readings.label: arbitrary_tradition
rejected_readings.rendering: The cultivated decide the regulation of words by an arbitrary, self-justifying tradition without any transcendent grounding.
rejected_readings.why: Misrepresents tradition as arbitrary and self-grounded, contradicting the commentary's explicit insistence that the smrti is not arbitrary but grounded in nibandhana and attachments to its source.

rejected_readings.label: arbitrary_grammar_tradition_only
rejected_readings.rendering: Grammar is an arbitrary, conventional system maintained by tradition alone, with no connection to any uncreated or transcendent principle.
rejected_readings.why: Simultaneously contradicts both axioms: (1) it denies grammar rests on uncreated teaching, and (2) it represents tradition as arbitrary, contradicting the commentary's insistence on nibandhana. It misses that both sources are necessary and neither is arbitrary.

notes: Verse 1.43 grounds grammar (śabdānāmanuśāsana) in two complementary sources: akṛtaka-śāstra (uncreated transcendent principle) and smṛti (tradition, given legitimacy through nibandhana—its grounded attachments to source). The contract captures the dual foundation and rules out both the reduction to a single source and the false claim that tradition or grammar is arbitrary.
